Pulsed field ablation of a persistent left superior vena cava triggering atrial fibrillation: A case report
abstract: We report the case of a 55-year-old Caucasian male undergoing a third catheter ablation procedure for atrial fibrillation (AF). During the intervention, a previously undiagnosed persistent left superior vena cava (LSVC) appeared to contribute to the maintenance of AF. Given its anatomical characteristics, electrical isolation of the LSVC was performed using pulsed field ablation (PFA) rather than conventional radiofrequency (RF) ablation. Pulsed field ablation seems to be a novel, effective, and safe tool, particularly for targeting larger anatomical structures.
summary: This case report describes a 55-year-old male with recurrent atrial fibrillation in whom pulsed field ablation successfully isolated a persistent left superior vena cava, demonstrating PFA as an effective alternative to conventional radiofrequency ablation for larger anatomical structures.

title: Isolated extravascular implantable cardioverter-defibrillator infection without leadless pacemaker involvement: A unique dual-device case report
abstract: Implantable cardioverter-defibrillators (ICDs) have been central in the prevention of sudden cardiac death (SCD) for over three decades (1–3). Despite their proven efficacy, traditional transvenous ICD (TV-ICD) systems carry substantial procedural and long-term risks, primarily due to the presence of intravascular leads (4,5). These complications mainly include lead malfunction, venous thrombosis, valvular injury and infection. Although relatively infrequent (0.6-1.3%), TV-ICD infections often require complete system removal and are associated with significant morbidity and mortality (6,7).
summary: This review discusses the long-standing clinical role of ICDs in sudden cardiac death prevention and highlights significant complications associated with traditional transvenous ICD systems, particularly lead-related infections (0.6-1.3% incidence) that often necessitate complete device removal with substantial morbidity and mortality. The content appears to be an introductory section setting the rationale for alternative ICD technologies rather than a complete study with defined methodology and results.

Utility of transesophageal echocardiography during implantation of leadless pacemaker in pediatric patients
abstract: Transcatheter leadless pacemakers (LP) eliminate pocket and lead-related complications associated with traditional transvenous pacing systems. The safety and efficacy of LP are well described in the adult population 1,2. Recently, pediatric studies have also found its use favorable in select patient populations 3. The LP is particularly useful in pediatric patients with transient or paroxysmal atrioventricular (AV) block and those with prolonged pauses leading to symptoms. Technical challenges unique to pediatric implantation include smaller patient size, smaller cardiac chamber size, proximity to the tricuspid (TV) and pulmonary valve (PV), and the need for adequate vein size of at least 9mm to safely accept the delivery system 4.
summary: This is a narrative review discussing the safety and efficacy of transcatheter leadless pacemakers in pediatric populations, particularly for transient/paroxysmal atrioventricular block and symptomatic prolonged pauses, while highlighting unique technical challenges including small patient size, cardiac anatomy constraints, and vascular access requirements (minimum 9mm venous diameter).

title: Rate-dependent transition from bradycardia-dependent atrioventricular block to inverse and conventional decremental conduction after transcatheter aortic valve replacement: A case report
abstract: Atrioventricular (AV) block is one of the major complications following transcatheter aortic valve replacement (TAVR).1 In recent years, only a few reports have described bradycardia-dependent AV block after TAVR.2 Among patients who develop intermittent left bundle branch block following TAVR, approximately 79.4% show bradycardia dependence, and both the intermittent and bradycardia-dependent patterns are reported to be more frequent than in non-TAVR patients.3 Similarly, intermittent, bradycardia-dependent AV block after TAVR may also occur more commonly than previously recognized.
summary: This is a retrospective observational review noting that bradycardia-dependent atrioventricular block occurs in approximately 79.4% of patients who develop intermittent left bundle branch block after TAVR, and such conduction abnormalities appear more frequent in TAVR patients than in non-TAVR populations, suggesting this complication may be underrecognized in clinical practice.

title: Peripheral endothelialization progression on fluoropolymer-coated left atrial appendage closure devices: Does device position influence healing?
abstract: The extent of endothelialization of left atrial appendage closure (LAAC) devices is a key determinant of thrombotic risk, particularly device-related thrombus (DRT) formation. Although animal models suggest that fluoropolymer (FP)-coated devices accelerate endothelial coverage and reduce thrombogenicity, clinical evidence remains limited. In particular, human data directly characterizing the extent, timing, and pattern of coverage are sparse, leaving the clinical relevance of FP-related acceleration unresolved.
summary: This retrospective observational study examines endothelialization patterns of left atrial appendage closure devices in humans, noting that while animal models suggest fluoropolymer-coated devices accelerate endothelial coverage and reduce device-related thrombus formation, direct clinical evidence characterizing the extent, timing, and pattern of coverage in patients remains limited. The authors aim to clarify the clinical relevance of fluoropolymer coating in reducing thrombotic complications, though specific sample size and key results are not provided in this abstract excerpt.
